First, AI-generated writing tends to sound like a lot of other AI-generated writing. Because it's drawing on the most common patterns it has seen, the output tends toward the average. It's competent. It's rarely surprising. Experienced teachers are getting better at recognizing this.

Second, AI can be wrong on factual details — including details in a homework assignment. If your child uses AI to research something and doesn't verify the information, they may submit something that sounds authoritative but contains errors.

Third, there's a real distinction between using AI to understand something and using AI to produce something. The parent's guide puts it well in Section 8:

"A useful question for parents to ask is: 'Could you explain this work without using the AI?'"

That's not a trick question. It's a genuine check. If your child can explain the ideas in their own words, they've learned something. If they can't, the AI may have done the learning for them.

A quick note on what AI is not

The parent's guide is clear on this: "AI systems do not think or understand the world the way humans do."

This is worth saying plainly, because a lot of media coverage treats AI as though it's either a genius or a monster. It's neither. It's a very powerful tool that is very good at certain things and genuinely unreliable at others.

It doesn't have opinions. It doesn't have feelings. It doesn't have goals. When it seems to, that's the pattern-matching at work — it has seen a lot of human text where people express opinions and feelings, so it produces text that sounds like that.

Understanding this doesn't make AI less useful. It makes it more useful, because you know what it's actually good for.

The short version:

  • AI works like a very sophisticated autocomplete — it predicts likely words, it doesn't "think."
  • This is why it can sound confident and still be completely wrong.
  • These errors (called hallucinations) are most common in citations, statistics, dates, and local or recent information.
  • The key question to ask your child: "Could you explain this work without using the AI?"
  • AI is a powerful tool. It's not a genius, and it's not a threat — it's a pattern-matcher with real limitations.

Now that you have a working mental model of what AI is, the next post gets more practical: we'll walk through the five main tools mentioned in the parent's guide, compare them in plain language, and help you decide which one is worth trying first.

Try it today: Open any AI tool from Post 1 and ask it about a topic you already know well — something from your job, your hobby, or your city. Read the response and notice one thing it gets slightly wrong or oversimplifies. That's the pattern-matcher at work.

Next up: Post 3 — ChatGPT, Copilot, Gemini, Claude, and Perplexity: A Plain-Language Comparison for Parents

Notes

  1. Google Cloud. "What Are AI Hallucinations?" Google Cloud, 2024. https://cloud.google.com/discover/what-are-ai-hallucinations ↩

  2. DataCamp. "AI Hallucinations: A Guide with Examples." DataCamp Blog, 2024. https://www.datacamp.com/blog/ai-hallucination ↩

entry.title: Part 1 - Before You Talk to Your Kids About AI, Try It Yourself
entry.summary: The best way to guide your children through the AI era is to try these tools yourself first. Here's how to get started in five minutes.
entry.content:
Part 1 of 12 in the Try It Yourself series — practical AI guidance for parents at blog.parentsguidetoai.ca

My very first experience with parenting and AI came from a day my daughter spent at Ethọ́s Lab. My daughter is very artistic, and routinely makes some absolutely fantastic art (I have a bias, but also she is actually really talented :D) That day she brought home an extremely complex piece of art that was obviously created digitally, but it was completely unlike anything she had ever made before. I already had a great deal of experience with Midjourney and Stable Diffusion, and almost immediately recognized that it was AI generated. She was insistent that she had created it herself and came up with the idea for it, but it took a while to get her to explain the tools she had used. She also got very frustrated because she had worked with the tool she used to get the result over multiple iterations. Back in early 2023, I was also a bit of an AI skeptic (and still remain adamantly attached to the idea that AI art isn't art because art requires human expression and intention). This conversation was the first time I had to think about how the the technology I was learning about AI and working with at work was going to impact parenting, and I was poorly prepared.

That's a moment most parents hit at some point, if you haven't already. Your kid mentions an AI tool you've never heard of. They ask you to explain what AI is. They give you a look. And you realize you're the least informed person in the conversation.

That moment is fixable, and it's really only takes a few minutes and some free tools.

I'm not suggesting you become an expert. You don't need to be. But there's a real difference between reading about AI and actually using it. This whole series is built on that idea: the best way to guide your children through the AI era is to try these tools yourself first.

I want to remind you, this blog, and my guide are a volunteer effort. I work with these technologies in my job every day, but I don't have a stake in promoting or using any tools. Like my guide, I don't use tracking technology on this blog, and I don't include referral links or get any rewards or sponsorships for the tools the I suggest here.

Why trying it yourself changes everything

Think about learning to drive. You can read every manual ever written about a car. But until you've actually sat behind the wheel, you don't really know what it feels like to merge onto a highway or parallel park on a hill. Advice from someone who's driven is just different from advice from someone who's read about it.

AI is the same way.

When you've used an AI tool, even just once, for five minutes, you will see and start to understand things that are hard to explain from the outside. You know that it sometimes sounds incredibly confident while being completely wrong. You know that the quality of what you get back depends heavily on how you ask the question. You know that it can feel strangely easy to trust.

Those are exactly the things your kids need to understand. And you're in a much better position to have that conversation if you've already been there.

When I had that conversation with my daughter, I had already discussed tools like Midjourney with friends who are artists, so I had already started to form opinions, so when I had to help her understand the difference between prompting a tool that creates a picture, and creating the picture herself, I was able to explain a bunch of the mechanics. Without that experience, it would have been difficult for me to discern where the picture had come from, and I may have operated under the assumption that she was just using a new drawing tool like Inkscape or Photoshop that she hadn't had access to before.

The parent's guide at parentsguidetoai.ca is a great starting point for understanding the landscape. But this series is designed to take you one step further — from understanding AI to actually using it.

The three easiest places to start

There are dozens of AI tools out there. For now, if you haven't used any before, ignore most of them. These three are the best starting points for parents, and they're all free:

ChatGPT (made by OpenAI) is probably the one you've heard the most about. It's a conversational AI — you type a question or a request, it responds. You can ask it to explain something, summarize an article, brainstorm ideas, or just answer a question. The free version is genuinely useful. Note that the minimum age for a personal account is 13.1

Microsoft Copilot is Microsoft's AI, and it's the one most directly relevant to Vancouver School Board families. The school version — Copilot 13+ — is a managed version with additional protections.2 The home version is free and runs in your browser without even needing an account to start. This is the one I'd suggest trying first if your child is a VSB student.

Google Gemini is Google's AI. If you use Gmail or Google Docs, you may have already seen it appear as a suggestion tool.3 The standalone version is free and works similarly to ChatGPT. It's particularly good at working with information and summarizing things.

All three are powerful and flexible tools. They're not identical — we'll go deeper on the differences in the next post. For now, just pick one.

What to expect from your first five minutes

Here's the thing about AI tools that nobody tells you: there's no wrong way to start. You won't break it. It doesn't judge you for asking something basic. And you don't need to prepare.

Just open one of the three tools above and type a question. Any question. Something you've actually been wondering about, or something related to your kids' school or schedule.

A few things you'll probably notice:

  • The response comes fast. Much faster than you might expect.
  • It sounds confident. This is worth paying attention to, because that confidence doesn't always mean it's right.
  • You can push back. If the answer doesn't make sense, or you want more detail, just say so. AI tools are designed for back-and-forth conversation.
  • It might get something wrong. That's okay. In fact, it's useful — we'll talk more about spotting mistakes in Post 5.

The first time I fired up ChatGPT I was enrolled in a script writing class, and I tried some simple things, like asking it to generate a script for a new scene based on Stargate SG-1, and then asked it to generate a list of references to past events in other Stargate episodes to tie in accuracy, and the whole thing it produced was a hot mess of inconsistent and hallucinated content. For me, it drove the point home early that these tools are not well designed research, consistency, or accuracy, although they can be useful with skill and experience.

The goal for this week is not to become an AI user. It's to stop being someone who's only read about it.

The short version:

  • You can guide your children better if you've tried AI tools yourself, even just once.
  • Trying the tools doesn't mean you have to use them, but you will have a better understanding of what they are and how they might be used.
  • Three free tools to start with: ChatGPT, Microsoft Copilot, and Google Gemini.
  • For VSB families, Copilot is the most directly relevant — it's a version of what your child may use at school.
  • There's no wrong way to start. Just open one and ask a question.
  • AI sounds confident. That doesn't always mean it's right.

Trying one of these tools this week will make the rest of this series more useful. Once you've had that first conversation with an AI, the next post will make a lot more sense: we're going to look at what AI actually is — and why understanding that matters when your child brings home AI-assisted work.

Try it today: Open Microsoft Copilot, ChatGPT, or Google Gemini and ask it one genuine question — something you actually want to know. That's it. Five minutes.

One final tip, if you do try the tools, consider trying three more things:

  • Ask a simple question about a task that is very mundane, for example, ask it to build a meal plan for you and your family for a week with a grocery list.
  • Ask a complex question about something you know very well. For example, if you are a mechanic, ask for a detailed explanation of how a two-stroke motor works. Look at the answer and note what it gets right, what it gets wrong, and what gets left out. Keep digging in with more questions to see if you can get it make mistakes.
  • Explain something that you want to accomplish that you don't know how to do, like creating a website, or planting a garden, but tell it to ask you questions about what you want to accomplish and to help build a plan to do that task.

Next up: Post 2 — What AI Actually Is (And Why That Matters When Your Child Brings Home AI-Assisted Work)

Notes

  1. OpenAI. "Is ChatGPT Safe for All Ages?" OpenAI Help Center, 2024. https://help.openai.com/en/articles/8313401-is-chatgpt-safe-for-all-ages ↩

  2. Vancouver School Board. "Copilot 13+ in Secondary Schools." Vancouver School Board, 2026. https://www.vsb.bc.ca/copilot-13-in-secondary-schools ↩

  3. Google. "Gemini Apps Help." Google Support, 2026. https://support.google.com/gemini/ ↩

entry.title: Introducing A Parent's Guide to Artificial Intelligence
entry.summary: Introducing A Parent's Guide to Artificial Intelligence — a free, plain-language resource for BC parents navigating AI in schools and at home.
entry.content:
I became a parent in 2009, and again in 2011, and learning to parent in a fully connected age, with cell phones, tablets, and social media has been a constant challenge, and still challenges me every day. I started giving public lectures in community and professional way back in 2003, well before I was married or had kids, but as my kids got into school and grew, I found myself shifting away from technical talks at conferences all over the world to giving lectures at schools and in community groups here in Vancouver.

A few years ago as the first iterations of modern LLMs and AI tools started becoming publicly available, I started getting questions from other parents. Not "what is AI?" — they'd heard plenty about that. The question was simpler and harder: "What do I actually do?"

What do I do when my kid comes home using tools I've never heard of? What do I do when the school sends a letter about a new AI policy and I don't understand what it means? What do I do if something goes wrong online and I'm not sure who to call?

I didn't have a good answer to give them. So I wrote one.

Today I'm sharing A Parent's Guide to Artificial Intelligence: In B.C. Schools and at Home — a free, openly licensed resource for parents, caregivers, and anyone supporting children through the AI era. You can also find the content on Github, all under a Creative Commons, Open Source license (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)

What the guide is

The guide is a plain-language resource for parents who don't have a technical background. It's not trying to convince you that AI is good or bad. It's trying to give you what you need to make informed decisions for your own family.

It covers what AI actually is - a tool that is available, and that can be used to learn, build, or explore new concepts and ideas. Like all new technologies, AI presents opportunities and challenges, and it takes time to learn and understand how they can be used. It covers the specific tools your children are likely using — including the Vancouver School Board's recent introduction of Microsoft Copilot for secondary students.1 It covers the real risks: privacy, misinformation, deepfakes, and the harms that can target young people online. And it covers what to do when something goes wrong, with specific BC and Canadian resources that are free and fast.

The guide is written to be accessible. I aimed for a Grade 8 reading level throughout — which means short sentences, plain words, and no unexplained jargon. There's a full glossary at the back for terms worth knowing.

It's also a living document. AI tools, school policies, and laws change quickly. The guide is published on GitHub with version history, so you can see what's changed and when. If you find something outdated or missing, you can open an issue and flag it.

What's inside

The guide is organized into sections you can read straight through or jump to as needed.

Understanding the basics — What AI actually is, how it works, and why the "it's just autocomplete" framing is more accurate than most news coverage. Why AI sounds confident even when it's wrong.

Vancouver School Board and Copilot 13+ — What the VSB's decision to introduce Microsoft Copilot for secondary students1 means in practice. What protections the school version includes. What questions to ask your school.

The opportunities — Where AI genuinely helps students: explaining difficult concepts, summarizing complex reading, supporting students with accessibility needs. How to encourage your child to use AI as a learning tool rather than a shortcut.

The risks — Privacy, misinformation, bias, copyright, deepfakes, non-consensual intimate images, sextortion, and emotional dependency. Each section is direct and practical — not alarmist, but honest about what's actually happening.

Helping your family — Conversation starters you can use tonight. A Family AI Agreement template. The AI Traffic Light: a simple green/yellow/red framework for thinking about different types of AI use.

If something goes wrong — Specific contacts and resources for BC families. Take Back Your Images, NeedHelpNow.ca, Cybertip.ca, ERASE, and Kids Help Phone. These are listed in one place so you're not searching during a crisis.

Appendices — A FAQ section answering the most common questions parents ask. A full list of Canadian resources and references. A glossary of AI and digital literacy terms. A quick-reference checklist.

Who it's for

The guide is written for parents and caregivers in British Columbia — especially Vancouver School Board families — but most of it applies anywhere in Canada, and the principles apply more broadly than that.

It's written for parents who aren't working in tech. If you're a developer or security professional, some sections will feel basic. That's intentional. This guide is for the parent who got a school newsletter about Copilot and wanted to understand what it meant before the parent-teacher night. It's written for the parent who finds out their child has been the victim of a deepfake, and it's written for the parent who finds out their child is the one making deepfakes.

I also want to be clear about something the guide states upfront: I have strong opinions about AI. Some uses of AI are positive, some are not. I made a deliberate editorial choice to set those aside in the guide, because parents need practical resources — regardless of where they land on the broader questions. Different families will reach different conclusions, and that's okay.

This blog series is where some of those opinions will come out. The guide tries to be neutral ground, but I will also share some of my opinions, and I may host guest posts that share differing opinions as well.

How the guide was made

I'm the primary author, but I used AI to help with parts of the process — specifically to automate the verification of sources, to review language and tone, and to adapt the structure for accessibility. I'm disclosing that because I think it matters, and because the guide itself talks about the difference between using AI as a tool and presenting AI output as your own work.

The content, the research, the editorial decisions, the organization, and the views expressed are mine. The AI helped with the mechanics, the layout, and even helped me build the scripts theme for this blog based on tools like Pelican, and building on an open source theme.

The guide is published under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 licence. You can share it, adapt it, and build on it — as long as you credit the source, don't use it commercially, and share any adaptations under the same licence. The source files are on GitHub.

If you do want to use it commercially, get in touch with me by opening an issue above, and we can try to figure out how that would work. If you want to use it as part of a course or article, we can work something out, If you are trying to sell my work, probably not!

What comes next

This announcement is also the start of something. I will be publishing a 12-part series — Try It Yourself: A Parent's Guide to Using AI designed to take parents one step further than the guide does. Over June and July I aim to publish one of the articles in the series every couple of days.

The guide explains the landscape. This series helps you actually use these tools yourself, so you can guide your children from experience rather than theory. We'll walk through the main AI tools, how to spot mistakes, how to protect your privacy, and how to have the conversations that matter.

You don't need to read the guide first to follow along. But if you want the full picture, the guide is at parentsguidetoai.ca. It's free, it's open, and it's a starting point — not a final word.

The guide is live now. If it's useful, share it with another parent who's trying to figure this out.

Read the guide: parentsguidetoai.ca

Report errors or suggest improvements: github.com/ygjb/parentsguidetoai.ca/issues

Start the blog series: Post 1 — Before You Talk to Your Kids About AI, Try It Yourself

Notes

  1. Vancouver School Board. "Copilot 13+ in Secondary Schools." Vancouver School Board, 2026. https://www.vsb.bc.ca/copilot-13-in-secondary-schools ↩↩
